data.title: Lensar And Alcon: Assessing Potential Outcomes
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Between now and the end of this year, LENSAR, Inc. (LNSR) is expected to be absorbed into competitor Alcon Inc. (ALC). That news came out on March 24, 2025, in a joint news release from both firms.

But until there is an official announcement that the deal has closed, there remains a possibility LENSAR will remain an independent company.

If the deal fails, should LENSAR shareholders remain invested, or should they look elsewhere? Alternatively, if the deal is consummated, should LENSAR shareholders consider Alcon shares to stay invested in the eye care industry?

About LENSAR And Alcon

Both firms are in the eye care business. LENSAR, with a market cap of $154.48 million, is a medical device firm. It specializes in laser systems for treating cataracts and the management of corneal astigmatism in the U.S.

Alcon, with a market cap of $43.37 billion, is a global, diversified eye care firm with its own cataract equipment and technology. For a company of this size, the acquisition of LENSAR was a minor transaction; terms of the deal were $14.00 per share in cash, along with a contingent value of up to $2.75 per share if LENSAR products deliver 614,000 cumulative procedures by December 31, 2027. The total deal has an implied value of $356 million.

News of the deal didn't impress investors, since the stock had closed at $14.24 the day before the announcement. And that came after the price finished at $16.87 the week before, on March 19. Since the announcement, the price has drifted down and floated between $13.00 and $14.00.

Prices below $14.00 signal that investors aren't sure the deal will be finalized. There's also likely some arbitraging going on, as speculators weigh the difference between $14.00 and the current price with the time remaining before finalization. For example, if I buy a share now for $13.16 and the deal closes on September 30, my return would be 6.38%, or 35.6% annualized. So, obviously, there is an arena for speculators here.

The LENSAR Situation

If the deal falls through and LENSAR continues on as an independent company, what can shareholders expect?

The following chart of revenue, net income, EBITDA, and basic EPS gives us a starting point:

The chart shows slow and relatively steady growth of revenue, while net income has been slipping since the second quarter of 2024; basic EPS on essentially the same slump, but EBITDA is rising quickly and quite steadily.

The obvious question is: What's happening between EBITDA and net income/EPS on the income statement?

Warrants. The company had issued warrants at a strike price of $5.00 per share and is obligated to buy them back at the current market price, whatever that is, until they expire. When the share price rises above the strike price as it has done, a liability is created, and the higher the share price, the greater the company's liability.

Back in Q1-2024, the share price usually traded in the $3.00 to $4.00 range, and the warrant obligation was negative, at $495,000 on the books. At the end of Q1-2025, shares traded at $14.12, and the obligation had risen to $21.74 million.

Without the warrant factor, the Q1-2025 loss would have been just $5.63 million versus the actual loss of $27.35 million. On an adjusted EBITDA, non-GAAP, basis, LENSAR was profitable.

Assuming the deal does not proceed, LENSAR will have this obligation hanging over its bottom-line results going forward. Of course, if the deal does not proceed, we might expect the current price to fall, and simultaneously bring down the warrants obligation.

Still, there's lots to like about the company. As I concluded in my February 5, 2025 article,

"With rapid growth of revenue and earnings since launching ALLY [a cataract treatment technology], its ability to take market share from other firms, and its innovative technology, it is well positioned for investors who like to invest in very small companies.

"It has a slew of Strong Buy ratings, and should reward long-term investors with robust capital gains for at least several years." [those Strong Buys are currently Holds]

In the Q1-2025 earnings report, data included (Q1-2024 results in brackets):

  • Revenue: $14.159 million ($10.588 million), an increase of 34%.
  • Net losses: $27.345 million ($2.162 million), or minus $2.32 this year versus minus $2.162 million last year.
  • Adjusted EBITDA: $0.2 million (minus $1.3 million); calculated by adding back stock-based compensation expense, change in the fair value of warrant liabilities, and acquisition-related costs.
  • Cash and cash equivalents: $25.2 million ($22.5 million at the end of 2024). That includes a $10 million cash deposit in connection with the Alcon deal.

Looking ahead, the Wall Street analysts see potentially large earnings gains this year and for the next two years:

These estimates assume LENSAR continues to exist as an independent entity.

I would add that adjusted EBITDA is likely to grow as revenue grows; it is not affected by the warrants liability. It will continue to be positive, and as a result, the company will have profits that could be reinvested in itself and growth.

To sum up, LENSAR's financial history is uninspiring, but I believe its potential for higher bottom-line results is strong.

The Alcon Situation

If the transaction is finalized, then shareholders of LENSAR could turn to Alcon to stay invested in the same industry. But should they?

Let's start again with a chart of revenue, net income, EBITDA, and EPS:

Investors will like this chart better than the one for LENSAR. Since coming out of a slump that began before COVID-19, all key fundamentals have grown quite steadily.

In 2018, it had an annual loss of $0.46; by 2021, it had turned its losses into net income, and it stayed profitable through to the present, with basic EPS of $2.26 in 2024. Wall Street analysts expect EPS to keep growing, somewhat slowly this year, but then by double digits per year over the next five years.

Given the steady gains on the top and bottom lines, these double-digit increases seem reasonable. Indeed, if the acquisition of LENSAR goes through, it will have a new source of revenue, and without the warrants liability, another source of net income and earnings.

Regarding the warrants, I expect they will be bought at the same price as common shares if/when the deal is finalized.

Price Scenarios

If the deal goes through, we can expect the LENSAR price to wind up at $14.00. The parties do not explain how the contingent $2.75 per share is to be allocated; in an all-cash deal, there will be no succeeding shares left to receive the $2.75. However, I think it is highly unlikely current shareholders will get it, or any part of it.

On the off chance the sale does not go through, I would expect LENSAR's share price to droop in the short term but rise in the longer term. In the short term, there might be disappointment, particularly among arbitrageurs. But in the longer run, we know the company has a significant share of the cataract treatment market (22.0%). It is generating profits through adjusted EBITDA and non-GAAP accounting. And, as we saw above, the price was $16.87 the week before the Alcon announcement.

If we assume that the price will, sooner or later, reflect earnings, then LENSAR's price could go much higher. Earnings are forecast to be 85.71% higher this year, and 70.51% higher in 2026. Since one quarter of 2025 is history, we will take three quarters of 85.71%, which is 64.28%. To that, we'll add one quarter of 70.51%, which is 17.63%. The sum of 64.28% and 17.63% is 81.91%.

To get a one-year price target, we will add 81.91% to the July 23 closing price of $13.16 for a total of $23.94. That's a big increase, but bear in mind it is theoretical, since the odds of the deal not closing are low.

The warrants should be included in these estimates since they are GAAP-based. However, the estimate sees a Q1 loss of $2.32 per share morphing into a loss of $0.39 per share. That's an improvement of about $2.00 per share, and possibly more if warrants are included, which they should be because they are liabilities. That's a significant gain and too much good news for me.

Finally, on this subject, an increase of 81.91% is obviously a very big increase. However, this increase is being added to a relatively low share price, so in dollar terms, it does not seem excessive. Watching a microcap jump by that amount is not unusual (of course, they can just as easily plunge by that amount).

As for buying Alcon to replace LENSAR, that's not likely to produce the same capital gains.

Alcon shares enjoyed a brief bump when the acquisition was announced, and that likely will happen again if/when the deal finalizes. Otherwise, I wouldn't expect much, if any, change. The LENSAR deal will provide a little more revenue and probably more on the bottom line, but their relative sizes matter. Alcon had 2024 revenue of $9.843 billion, while LENSAR had $14.2 million.

And, LENSAR is just one of several recent acquisitions. Two days after the LENSAR deal, Alcon announced in a news release that it had purchased, for an undisclosed sum, Aurion Biotech, Inc., and on July 7, 2025, it announced it would acquire LumiThera, Inc., again for an undisclosed price.

Using the same pricing strategy, the Wall Street analysts expect Alcon's earnings to increase 2.30% this year and 19.42% next year. Three quarters of 2.30% is 1.73%, and one quarter of 19.42% is 4.86%.

Adding the two results gives us 6.59%, and that amount applied to the July 23 closing price of $92.22 provides a one-year target of $98.30.

Since I consider the odds in favor of a closure, I would rate LENSAR a hold; at this point it's best suited to speculators. If the deal falls through, then I would rate it a buy for the potential capital gains. The Seeking Alpha system also has a hold, as do the Quant system, and Wall Street analysts.

Risk Factors

Investing with the assumption the deal will not go through could be a problem. Both parties agreed to the deal, so the odds are high it will proceed. There are always minor complications in such deals, though, so a failure is not impossible.

If the deal falls through, LENSAR investors should analyze the tradeoff between the potential of high capital gains and the knowledge that warrant liabilities could depress returns.

My estimates of future prices and the EPS forecasts of the Wall Street analysts are based on the assumption that external political and economic conditions in the near future will be about the same as those in the recent past.

Conclusion

In the Investment Thesis section, I asked two questions: should LENSAR investors stay invested in it if the deal falls through? The answer to that is yes, since the company has the potential to deliver strong capital gains over the next 18 months.

Second, if the deal is concluded, should LENSAR shareholders turn to Alcon to maintain a presence in the eye care industry? Aggressive investors, which I assume are well represented among LENSAR investors, should look elsewhere. Alcon is a mature, large cap and is unlikely to deliver exceptional returns. For other investors, Alcon is worth considering, especially since its top and bottom lines have grown steadily for several years.

data.title: What Breadcrumbs From NextEra Disclosures Mean For Eos Energy
data.description:
Background

Eos Energy Enterprises (NASDAQ:EOSE) is a development-scale company now coming into strong revenue-producing grid-scale batteries based on a non-flammable zinc-bromine chemistry. Most grid-scale batteries today are based on flammable lithium-ion technologies, with significant parts of their supply chain tied up in China. NextEra (NYSE:NEE) is the largest utility in the United States with a significant backlog in solar, storage, and renewables. The company has stated that the quickest technology to market at the moment is solar + storage.

What breadcrumbs has NextEra dropped that could affect EOS

A video hosted on Politico with John Ketchum, CEO of NextEra, is a great breadcrumbs resource. One amazing quote from the interview is as follows, per this author's transcription:

We've been able to source all of our batteries from a company right here in the US, being able to decouple almost entirely from buying from Asian suppliers. (minute 16:13 from video, author's emphasis added)

Other great moments in this video and approximate time stamps include:

  • Gas turbines are backlogged 6-7 years and have recently tripled in price (~min 4)
  • Solar and Storage are the fastest sources to bring online (~min 19)

Here's a satellite image of the Torrecillas site partially deployed:

The image shows about 13 Z3 containers deployed at the lower right in the image, occupying only part of the pads. A subsequent satellite pass confirmed that all of these units had been removed. EOS IR responded to this author that they had been removed as the customer wanted all containers of exactly the same type. The company later trumpeted its speed of installing all 75 Z3 cubes in just seven days from truck to pad, the so-called "cold commissioning." This is a rate that Lithium Ion competitors can't match, given the installation of elaborate fire suppression systems they require, etc.

So I think EOS could be the company in the quote, "We've been able to source all of our batteries from a company right here in the US" (emphasis added). One risk is that Ketchum was being hyperbolic when he said "...all of our batteries". Clearly, NextEra has used lithium-ion batteries for its previous installations, since EOS's automated production line has only recently come up to speed. And there are some things that the EOS Z3 can't do that lithium does extremely well, like discharge most of its capacity in an hour or two. The Z3's round-trip efficiency doesn't meet that of lithium until the discharge rates get to the 8-16 hour range (a discharge duration lithium can't economically meet). Finally, there is no other grid-scale company, of which this author is aware, that has a 91% domestic supply chain.

In fact, combining short-duration lithium and long-duration EOS Z3s would be complementary and not competitive in many installations. But let's take Ketchum at his word that he is sourcing all of his batteries from the only grid-scale domestic manufacturer with 91% domestic content.

And what does this mean for EOS?

Today, EOS has one production line fully online. They have ordered a second line and are doing heavy building modifications to put it in place, probably by year-end with commissioning starting around then. Each automated line can produce about 2 GWh per year, or $500 million in revenue.

To understand what NextEra's backlog may mean for EOS, one needs to understand the potential contribution of an incremental line to EOS's valuation. I do a quick horseback guess as follows: Take $500 million in incremental revenue. Apply a 50% gross margin (leading brokerage and company underwriter Stifel is at 40.6% for 2026 and 56.9% for 2027). Then apply Stifel's numbers for SG&A (15.9% of revenue) and R&D (3.8%), and $0.28 cents per fully diluted share (538 million from most current estimates) drops out of the bottom of the horseback guess quickie model. Apply a 35x PE, which might be appropriate for a market leader that is quickly growing, and $10/share for each additional line seems to be the number. I've seen pretty well justified estimates; this could be as high as $13/share for each incremental production line.

And what does NextEra's storage backlog look like?

On their earnings call for Q2 on July 23, 2025, the company released not just breadcrumbs on the implications for EOS but perhaps an entire bakery. Their earnings presentation is at this link. One of my favorite slides is this one:

Near the bottom of the left-hand column, NextEra states they have added 0.9 GW of firm storage backlog since Q1. Since EOS batteries can discharge over 8 hours, this would equate to 7.2 GWh hours of storage. And this would require 3.6 additional lines to produce. Per the math of $10/incremental production line, this would be a bump of $36 to EOS's share price.

An even more impressive slide was deeper in the presentation deck:

The bottom right figure indicates they have 5.2 GW of storage in development for the 2026–2027 timeframe. Applying a typical discharge rate of 8 hours, one gets 41.6 GWh. And that would take 20+ additional production lines to produce. And at $10 per incremental line, that is a $200 bump to the share price. Not bad for a company trading at $6 in July 2025!

What about EOS's short-term earnings?

EOS presents their Q2 results on Wednesday, 7/30/25, after the close, followed by a call the next morning. The company has been silent as the sphinx on their sales funnel lately. But I believe they may report $25-$30 million in revenue for the quarter, which is almost triple Q1. An EPS estimate would require all the mark-to-market for their derivative securities, so I will leave it there for now.

Risks to my thesis:

  • As cited above, NextEra may not indeed be buying all of its batteries from EOS. On the other hand, NextEra proudly cites its speed to bring project lines up quickly, and EOS's Z3 has demonstrated awesome "cold commissioning" time as cited above. Z3 is not install and forget but is far more hands-off once commissioned than lithium-ion, which requires swapping out of degraded units as early as five years after installation, constant maintenance, and safety checks on fire suppression systems, and the like.
  • A second risk is that the payback per line may not equate to $10/share. No interest for the cap ex on each line is included, for instance. But large customers may pay for a line to have it dedicated to their projects. On the positive side of payback, the company has indicated pricing may be better than $250/kw, R&D is not likely to scale up linearly with each line, and margins, as cited by a knowledgeable underwriter, may be better.
  • EOS may experience hiccups in bringing up additional lines. This is a new process, and only one line exists to date. While EOS battery content is primarily domestic, as the company has said, I suspect that percentage isn't as high for all the production equipment. A supply chain tangle or tariffs could slow down parts of the automated line deliveries.

Conclusions

EOS is a development-stage company just coming into revenue, and as such, there are huge execution and timing risks. But their recent endorsements and loans from Cerberus and the DOE and their very recent 7% equity investment by BlackRock speak loudly to me. So does the enormous and almost unslakable thirst for power that AI centers demand, along with NextEra's CEO Ketchum's assertion that solar plus storage is one of the cheapest, best, and quickest ways to solve this demand. In the last month, the NY Times has carried an article on Amazon's Indiana AI data center with its 2.2 GW power requirement. And OpenAI has announced Stargate will require 4.5 GW in power. To put those numbers in perspective, the peak demand for San Francisco is 1 GW.

This article has only concentrated on NextEra's US backlog and its implications for EOS. Obviously, there are lots of other utilities and parts of the world where EOS already has units on test. My first SA article on the company, "A Gridscale Battery in a Trillion Dollar Market" detailed a $1 trillion total addressable market. That may now be too low, given the accelerating adoption of AI worldwide, with its voracious power needs.

I believe Cerberus, DOE, and BlackRock's cash and endorsements are worthy. And I believe Ketchum is correct that solar + storage is the best way to meet demand. And I believe EOS will do quite well.

data.title: Silver: The Convergence Point Between 3 Structural And Accelerating Trends
data.description:
Description

I am writing this article as silver prices are hovering around $40.00 per ounce for the first time since the fall of 2011. According to the Silver Institute, the global silver market has been in a supply deficit since 2021, and with industrial demand in structural growth, the ongoing tightness in the physical market is unlikely to go away anytime soon. Given the highly fragmented production base, there is simply no way to meaningfully increase supply in the foreseeable future. After researching most of the publicly listed silver producers, I would say that simply maintaining the global primary mine production of ~835 million ounces is already an achievement in itself.

In general terms, I would say that I am quite disappointed by the quality of the primary silver assets available in the market today. To my eyes, MAG Silver (MAG) was probably among the best financial instruments to gain exposure to the metal, but with the ongoing $2.1 billion transaction with Pan American Silver (PAAS), it is unlikely to be listed for much longer. In terms of quality, I remember being also impressed by Endeavour's (EXK) Terronera Project, expected to produce 7.0 million silver equivalent ounces at an all-in sustaining cost of $2.15 per ounce. I will continue to monitor the opportunities available in the marketplace, but at least for now, I see little incentive to move away from my core bullion holdings. In terms of paper silver, we would then be talking about the iShares Silver Trust (NYSEARCA:SLV), the Sprott Physical Silver Trust (NYSEARCA:PSLV) or the abrdn Physical Silver ETF (NYSEARCA:SIVR).

After researching MAG Silver, I knew that Juanicipio was an exceptional asset, and even within Fresnillo's portfolio, it stands out. If the transaction with MAG Silver is ultimately completed, I think Pan American is making an excellent move in acquiring MAG's 44% ownership interest in Juanicipio. Yes, the $2.1 billion price tag may look elevated today, but considering silver's highly compelling supply and demand fundamentals, it could look pretty cheap a few years from now. Fresnillo: Juanicipio Standing Out Once Again

As discussed in detail in a previous article, one of the key differences between holding physical bullion versus paper silver purchased via ETFs is the counterparty risk associated with clearinghouses like the Depository Trust & Clearing Corporation. My goal on this platform is not to comment on whether this risk is about to materialize or not, but suffice it to say that it is present. If you want a better understanding of the counterparty risks involved, I invite you to simply pay close to the DTCC's balance sheet with an equity of $4.5 billion while having in excess of $100 trillion of assets into custody.

At the end of the day, everyone owning financial assets like stocks and bonds is exposed to counterparty risks with central clearing parties. Is it right, is it wrong? To me, it is just a fact. Everyone owning paper assets is implicitly making a bet on the balance sheet of entities like the DTCC. How risky is such a bet? Again, everyone will have to answer this question for themselves. My goal is to ask hard questions that few people in this frothy market are thinking about. The Rise Of Physical Gold And Silver Over Paper Assets

Short-term market movements are notoriously difficult to forecast, but I have the feeling that once the $40.00 threshold is broken, the move to $50.00 could happen pretty quickly. This is the famous resistance line still in place since the highs of the 1980 bull market. After 45 years and a 40-fold increase in the U.S. federal debt, silver prices are still trading 20% below their historical highs. How incredible is that! I don't know how long the battle around the $50.00 Maginot Line will last, but I know that once it is broken, we could witness some of the most violent moves ever seen in the precious metal space.

Just remember what happened in January 2025 when the London Bullion Market Association was forced to lengthen gold delivery times from the typical 2-3 days to 4-8 weeks. If there is visible tightness in the physical gold market, the situation is likely to be even more explosive on the silver side of the equation. The key difference is that silver is actually consumed in numerous industrial processes, in addition to the demand from investment purposes. With structural demand growth from industrial usage, what happens as over-the-ground inventories continue to thin out? What is the value of a futures contract on the COMEX if there is a crisis of confidence about its actual convertibility into physical metal? In the spring of 2020, we saw oil prices in negative territory, so could we see a similar situation with silver in the midst of a delivery crisis? Although no one knows all the details of how it could play out, I do know that the current supply and demand fundamentals are unsustainable, and the longer they persist, the higher the probability of an explosion somewhere within the ecosystem.

In the following paragraphs, my goal is to highlight why the current supply deficits are unlikely to go away anytime soon and why they could actually worsen quite dramatically over the next decade or so. With multiple structural trends supporting demand combined with inelastic production volumes, it is my personal view that silver may be among the most undervalued assets in the world today.

Catalyst #1: Liquidity Issues on the U.S. Treasury Market

Given the precarious fiscal position of the United States, the perception of safety around U.S. Treasuries is shifting. The confidence game could reach a critical point in the case of a breakout above 5.0% on 10-year yields, and in such a situation, the United States could have its own Liz Truss moment. Except that this time around, it would be on the largest and most liquid market in the world; the bedrock of the monetary system in place since the end of World War II. What would be the impacts of such an event on demand for physical delivery? Needless to say that it could significantly enhance capital outflows from paper assets such as U.S. Treasuries, and into hard assets like physical gold and silver. Given the amount of paper outstanding relative to the amount of physical metal available for delivery, this is akin to a worldwide game of musical chairs. In my view, it is only in the midst of a delivery crisis that real price discovery in the silver market is likely to occur.

The physical silver market is already in severe deficit, so how could it accommodate a sudden surge in investment demand in response to liquidity issues on the U.S. Treasury market? This is when we could start to see the inelasticity of silver prices play out. As illustrated below, more than 70% of global silver production comes as a by-product of base and precious metals mines, meaning that an increase in silver prices may not necessarily lead to an increase in supply. In the case of a large copper mine, what would be the overall impact on production volumes if silver prices were to double? Would it justify the deployment of billions in capital to increase mining and milling capacity? Moreover, could you justify investment decisions measured in decades based on a spike in the prices of a by-product? In both instances, a rapid increase in silver prices is unlikely to have any significant impact on future production volumes, at least in the short to medium term.

In addition, the mining industry must also deal with 800 million ounces of depletion every single year. The Juanicipio Mine mentioned previously is one of the world's largest primary silver mines, and this is an asset with ~122 million ounces of proven and probable silver. Based on current production volume, the global mining industry must find the equivalent of ~6.5 Juanicipio every single year just to offset depletion. In the midst of structural demand growth, where will the metal come from?

Catalyst #2: Structural Growth From Photovoltaic Applications

One of the most important trends within the silver industry over the last decade or so was the rise in demand from photovoltaic applications. As illustrated below, demand from photovoltaic increased from 81.6 million ounces in 2016 to a forecast of almost 200 million ounces in 2025, the equivalent of a ~10% CAGR over this 9-year window.

Could the continued growth of photovoltaic applications add another 100 million ounces of silver demand over the next decade? Based on BloombergNEF forecasts, the amount of photovoltaic new build in the next decade could actually be much larger than what we have witnessed in past. According to SolarPower Europe, the world installed a record 597 GW of solar in 2024, a number that could reach 1 TW by 2030. Those projections are even more impressive when considering that only 243 GW of solar capacity were added in 2022, with China being the largest contributor by far.

In this context, should we be surprised that supply deficits in the silver market became apparent precisely at the same moment, around 2021 and 2022? This is when the growth in photovoltaics really kicked in after reaching a critical mass. If the last few years have already put significant strain on the physical silver market, just imagine what is about to occur between now and 2030. Whether or not the world will reach 1 TW in new build capacity by 2030 is not important, as what truly matters is the overall trend. Photovoltaic applications will continue to act as a source of structural silver demand, and this is true as far as the eye can see.

Catalyst #3: The Rise of Solid-State Batteries

The importance of solid-state batteries on silver demand today is probably akin to the photovoltaic industry back in the early 2010s. At the time, photovoltaics had considerable potential, but in terms of tangible silver demand, it was essentially a rounding error.

At the SNE Battery Day 2024 held in Seoul, Samsung unveiled new solid-state battery technologies that could have a dramatic impact on the electric vehicle industry in the medium to long term. Samsung's new technology will first be used in premium EVs, giving them up to 621 miles of range. But more importantly, this new solid-state battery could recharge to 80% in just nine minutes, which solves one of the biggest issues of the EV transition. With an estimated useful life of 20 years, solid-state batteries are also expected to be much more durable than the current lithium batteries, and that comes on top of a much higher energy density.

I have never researched the lithium industry in detail, but given where Albermale (ALB) and Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile S.A. (SQM) are trading at, it is certainly an industry that I would like to know more about. Considering all the promises made by new solid-state technologies, what could be the potential impacts on lithium demand? As of now, I don't pretend to have the answer, but this is a question I could explore in future articles.

With Samsung's new solid-state technology, approximately 1 kilogram of silver would be required to manufacture a typical vehicle's 100 kWh battery pack. Based on the assumption that 20% of global car production ultimately ends up with a solid-state battery, we could then be talking about additional silver demand of approximately 500 million ounces per year. Solid-state technologies are still in an early stage of deployment, so the impact is unlikely to be felt anytime soon, but we can see how new storage technologies could rapidly become the elephant in the room when it comes to physical silver demand.

Even with a penetration rate of only a few percentage points, solid-state batteries could easily add another 100 million ounces to overall silver demand. This would be in addition to the increasing demand for investment and usage in photovoltaic applications. Again, the silver market is already in severe supply deficits as of today, so what will accommodate the continuous increase in demand?

Conclusion

Over the past few weeks, I have researched the vast majority of publicly listed silver equities. I still have a few names to investigate, but the picture is already fairly clear, meaning that just maintaining primary silver production around 850 million ounces per year over the next decade will already be a massive achievement in itself.

The largest silver-producing mine that is expected to come into production in the next few years is Skeena's (SKE) Eskay Creek, located in the Golden Triangle of British Columbia. Over its mine life, Eskay Cree is expected to produce an average of 6.6 million ounces of silver annually, which is not even 1% of today's global production.

Endeavour's Terronera Mine is another important addition to the global supply base, but once again, we are talking about 4.0 million ounces of additional silver per year, or about 0.5% of the worldwide supply. We could also talk about some development projects like Aya's (AYA:CA) Boumadine Project in Morocco or Avino's (ASM) La Preciosa Project, but in the grand scheme of things, the expected production from those two assets is a rounding error for the industry as a whole.

As we look into the exploration side of the business, we find names like Highlander Silver (HSLV:CA) and Dolly Varden Silver (DVS) offering compelling drilling targets, but even a massive discovery made today would be unlikely to reach production for at least another decade. Given the timeframe involved with exploration, it is unlikely to be of much help in answering today's supply deficits.

In my view, the only way to meaningfully increase silver production in the coming years would be through recycling. Over the last decade, this production added approximately 50 million ounces per year to global supply, so even in this case, it would not be enough to support the structural demand growth associated with electrification. However, in case silver prices finally surpass $100 per ounce or more, this is where the greenspot in the supply landscape would be.

data.title: Compass Group: Bids Big On Vermaat
data.description:
In my last post, I honestly thought Compass Group (OTCPK:CMPGY) (OTCPK:CMPGF) shares were due for a breather.

I mean, they were already up 150% over the past five years.

After leading the pack in global contract foodservice growth since the pandemic, the stock was trading at pretty stretched valuations. Compare only with direct peers like Aramark (ARMK), for example. It is trading at an EV/EBITDA of +15x and an EV/EBIT of +20x, around 23% and 21% higher than its American peer.

That said, if you check out the chart on the side, it's clear Compass Group has outpaced the S&P 500 since my last post. The stock’s up just over 14.6%, and that bumps up to 15.4% with dividends included—compared to a 10.1% return from the index over the same stretch.

But look, if we hopped in a time machine and went back just two or three days, Compass was actually trailing the S&P.

What happened then? Of course, the British giant dropped its Trading Update. But to me, that near-double-digit rally we just saw comes down to a few reasons.

Here’s what I think drove it:

  1. Organic revenue growth of 8.6% year-over-year was stronger than market expectations (9.6% year-over-year in NA and 6.6% year-over-year in International). This was driven by both major hospitality events and retention (96%). Although not disclosed, net revenue growth remained in the mid-range of 4% to 5%.
  2. Acquisition of Vermaat, a Dutch company that specializes in personalized food concepts for on-site dining and large-scale delivery. Compass scooped it up for €1.5 billion.
  3. Acceleration in full-year growth outlook. That is, they now expect at least 8% year-over-year organic revenue growth (up to $45.3 billion) and 11% year-over-year EBIT (approximately $3.24 billion, an EBIT margin of approximately 7.15%).

Taking all this into account, I think there are two key things to focus on (since this is just a Trading Update and we don't have the full financials yet): dig a little deeper into the Vermaat acquisition and revisit my valuation.

After all, now we have a more positive outlook ahead.

Consequences of the Vermaat Acquisition

There's been a lot of buzz around Vermaat—from its impressive 15-year revenue CAGR of nearly 20% to its margins sitting in the top quartile of the industry.

But as we all know, anything that is good usually comes with a hefty price tag.

The acquisition of this company, for example, came at 20x EBIT, which is approximately €1.5 billion (or $1.77 billion if we consider an exchange rate of 1 euro to 1.18 US dollars).

By inference, we can say that Vermaat generated an annual EBIT of up to $88.5 million. Assuming it continues to grow at a CAGR of nearly 20%, we would see EBIT of approximately $106.2 million coming from there by FY 2026, surpassing $219.9 million in FY 2030.

Another very important piece of information gleaned from the Q&A is that the company generates revenues of up to €500 million annually ($590 million), double that of Compass Group's current operations in the Netherlands.

It's worth pointing out that this suggests Vermaat's EBIT margin sits around 15%, which is way above Compass Group's own margin, hovering around 5% on average over the past five years.

But how would they finance this?

Even if they generate FCF adjusted for other investing activities of over $1.1 billion, we would likely see an acceleration in leverage. Currently, the debt/EBITDA ratio is less than 1.5x and should exceed that after the transaction, but not exceed 2x.

In this position, they could (as they said they are considering these possibilities) increase Capex and/or consolidate even more players, stretching it as needed, and reevaluating it around FY 2027.

No problem regarding this debt.

As I said in my last post, they are at extremely healthy levels when it comes to capital structure.

The bad news is that they don't plan on repurchasing their shares until FY 2027, when they will likely deleverage. Only then will they decide whether to accelerate their growth or consolidate more companies in their portfolio.

Cheap Compared to Aramark?

You really can't talk about Compass Group without Aramark coming to mind. What's interesting is that when you look at valuation metrics like EV/EBIT, the two have pretty much always traded side by side.

Just take a look at this and see if it's not very similar over the last year:

Remember, the company implicitly guided us to an EBIT of at least $3.24 billion. We'll use Aramark's EV/EBIT multiple (roughly 17x) and the following assumptions in our model for the end of FY 2025:

  1. Approximately 1.7 billion shares outstanding at year-end (without buybacks, as indicated by management).
  2. A small increase in net debt. Since they're targeting a 1.5x debt-to-EBITDA ratio, I imagine they should increase their debt to somewhere between $3.7 and $3.8 billion. That said, around $3 billion of net debt is reasonable, in my opinion.

Based on these assumptions, we land on a price target between $30 and $31—which is still at least 11% below where the stock is trading right now. Sure, that’s a slight bump from our previous target, but calling it a ‘Buy’ at this point feels like a bit of a stretch.

Another way to look at this is that Compass trades at a 30% premium to Aramark for EV/EBITDA. Currently, Aramark trades at a 12.3x multiple; we could imagine a multiple of up to 16x for Compass.

Assuming EBITDA growth of 11%, we would have a price target of approximately $3.74 billion for FY 2025. By applying the same assumptions I made for EV/EBIT into the model, we arrive at a price target of between $33 and $34.

This is literally on top of Wall St. consensus estimates.

With a 1.7% dividend yield and almost no upside under these circumstances, I maintain my 'hold' rating on Compass. However, I'm raising my price target to something closer to the low $30s.

This will be interesting to see.

Unfortunately, I didn't capture certain nuances, such as inorganic growth drivers and the premium under Aramark's valuation. This caused us to overlook some upside, which ended up outperforming the S&P.

There's no point in crying over spilled milk now, but I don't believe this is an opportunity at this point. If the price corrects to something closer to the high $20s, I feel it would be an opportunity to capture an upside of at least 25%, factoring in the dividends.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Raymond James: Rising Markets Offset Cash Headwinds (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Raymond James Financial (RJF) have been a very strong performer over the past year, gaining about 44%. Rising markets have supported higher assets under management ("AUM") and associated fees, while higher-for-longer interest rates support net interest income. I last covered shares in February, arguing favorable fundamentals were already reflected in valuation, rating shares a "hold." Since then, the stock is about 3% lower, near my $164 fair value estimate, consistent with my view that the upside was priced in. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit RJF.

Q3 Was Distorted By A One-Time Item

In the company's fiscal third quarter, Raymond James earned $2.18, which missed expectations by $0.18 even as revenue grew by 5% to $3.4 billion. While it is never encouraging to see a company miss estimates, there was a unique one-time item that drove the variance. RJF took a $58 million legal reserve related to its role underwriting bonds on a specific issuer in the 2013-2015 timeframe. Rather than defend itself in court, RJF decided to settle the case, while accepting no wronging. This reduced EPS by over $0.20. Absent this, earnings were just slightly ahead of consensus.

In the quarter, asset management fees rose by 8% to $1.7 billion, while securities commissions grew 4% to $431 million. Compensation expenses rose by 5%, in line with revenue, to $2.2 billion. Most of its financial advisors receive a payout tied to the advisory fees and commissions they generate, and so compensation should move broadly in line with revenue.

Client Cash Remains A Pressure Point

One headwind to the business has been that its clients have been deploying more cash into markets or into higher yielding fixed income alternatives. As a result, client cash sweep assets were down 4% sequentially to $55 billion. As you can see, cash sweep assets are down about $1.2 billion over the past year, and cash utilization is down substantially from 4.3% to 3.8%.

This has been a meaningful headwind to results because while RJF manages money, sells financial products, and provides investment banking advisory services, earning interest on its clients' cash is ultimately the way this company makes money. RJF generated $546 million of net interest income, up from $523 million last year. However, its third-party bank fees were $110 million from $149 million as yields on this program shrank from 3.41% to 2.96%. Across the two ways it monetizes client cash, it earned $656 million.

Now, the company had pre-tax income of $563 million across its business segments. In other words, it loses about $93 million outside of its cash revenue. Even factoring in its $58 million legal reserve, it lost about $38 million ex-cash, and this is consistent with trends. Adjusted pre-tax margin compressed 360 bps to 17.1% as cash economics worsened. Depending on the quarter, RJF swings from modest gains or losses ex-cash, but cash economics are the primary driver of its results. It will be important to see cash balances begin to stabilize, especially as potential Fed rate cuts are likely to compress interest margins next year.

Rising Markets Have Boosted Results

Drilling into results by segments, in its Private Client Group, where Raymond James houses its financial advisors, assets rose by 11% to $1.57 trillion, aided by market gains. Revenue was up 3% from last year to $2.49 billion, as strength in fees was offset by cash headwinds. As a result of this pressure, this unit earned $411 million, down 7% from last year.

While higher markets drove much of its asset growth, net new assets were $11.7 billion in the quarter, which is a 3.4% annualized growth rate. So far this fiscal year, it has generated $34.5 billion of NNA, or a 3.3% growth rate, down from $48 billion last year, a 5.8% growth rate. This is a meaningful slowdown in growth. While management sees a strong pipeline of potential advisor wins, we are not yet seeing it impact results.

Still, looking ahead, we should see a meaningful acceleration in revenue in the fiscal fourth quarter. Advisory fees are charged on a lag, typically based on quarter-end levels. With the S&P rallying from ~5,600 on March 31st to ~6,200 on June 30th, advisory revenue should rise substantially in fiscal Q4. This should be at least a $100 million revenue tailwind, though after paying increased commissions to advisors, the net income impact will be closer to $30 million.

Elsewhere, Capital Markets reported a $54 million loss, given its legal reserve, even as revenue grew by 15% to $381 million. Absent this one-time item, this unit would have generated a $4 million profit, still a subdued result. It did see an increase in investment banking due to increased underwriting activity, as capital markets loosened. Still, M&A activity remains weak. Based on its pipeline, management expects the second half of the calendar year to be better in investment banking than the first half, even as the outlook is still mixed.

Its asset management arm grew earnings by 12% to $125 million as revenue jumped by 10% to $291 million as AUM rose to $263 billion, up 15% from a year ago. Meanwhile, bank earnings were up 7% to $123 million. It provisioned $15 million for credit losses and had just $3 million of charge-offs. Nonperforming assets were stable at 0.34%, and reserves are 2.73x nonperforming assets, above my 2.5x threshold of strong coverage. This unit has a very high-quality loan of about $50 billion that should have limited losses. Its bank has a 13.1% tier 1 leverage ratio, which is extremely strong.

Capital Returns Can Push Shares Higher

Beyond client cash, RJF has $2.3 billion of corporate cash. This further bolsters its capital position, and it is well above RJF's ~$1.2 billion target, providing it with room for M&A or capital return. Indeed in Q3, the company did $451 million of share repurchases up from $250 million in the prior quarter to take advantage of the decline in the share price amidst tariff-related volatility. Given its strong capital position, it expects buyback activity to continue at this elevated pace as disclosed on the earnings call.

Back in February, I saw Raymond James earning about $10.00-$10.50 this year, leaving it with about $7.60 of capital return capacity, given the need to retain about a quarter of earnings over the medium-term to support growth. In the near-term with about $12 of excess capital per share, RJF can return at least 100% of earnings.

Excluding the legal settlement, I see RJF earning $10.40-$10.55 this year as the higher market will provide a bit of a boost to advisory revenue in Q4. Looking to 2026, assuming an S&P 500 of at least 6,200 and 3 Fed rate cuts over the next 12 months, I would expect RJF to earn $10.75-$11.25, depending on the level of cash balances.

That gives the company ~$8.25 of run-rate capital return capacity, enabling buybacks on top of its 1.25% dividend yield. I continue to view a ~5% capital return yield as the appropriate valuation, given ~5% long-term growth and a 10% required rate of return. That argues for a forward fair value of ~$165. RJF is also carrying about $10 of excess capital for a go-forward fair value of $175.

This $11 increase from 5 months ago primarily reflects the passage of time as the company generates organic growth, a lower share count from buybacks, as well as benefits from a higher market level boosting AUM and associated revenue. I see the primary risk being either more Fed rate cuts or a continued decline in client cash assets, which could reduce EPS by about $1.00.

With shares below $160 in response to earnings, RJF now has ~11% upside to my fair value on top of its dividend. As such, after their recent underperformance, I am moving shares up to a "buy," as this has created a sufficient opportunity in shares. While a sharper decline in rates is a risk, I do not expect faster Fed cuts given inflation, and I would begin adding RJF.
